elements.sent-2411-001: Effective training of large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on resource-constrained client devices in Federated Learning (FL) requires the usage of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategies.
elements.sent-2411-002: To this end, we demonstrate the impact of two factors viz., client-specific layer importance score that selects the most important VLM layers for fine-tuning and inter-client layer diversity score that encourages diverse layer selection across clients for optimal VLM layer selection.
elements.sent-2411-003: We first theoretically motivate and leverage the principal eigenvalue magnitude of layerwise Neural Tangent Kernels and show its effectiveness as client-specific layer importance score.
elements.sent-2411-004: Next, we propose a novel layer updating strategy dubbed F$^3$OCUS that jointly optimizes the layer importance and diversity factors by employing a data-free, multi-objective, meta-heuristic optimization on the server.
elements.sent-2411-005: We explore 5 different meta-heuristic algorithms and compare their effectiveness for selecting model layers and adapter layers towards PEFT-FL.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we release a new MedVQA-FL dataset involving overall 707,962 VQA triplets and 9 modality-specific clients and utilize it to train and evaluate our method.
elements.sent-2411-007: Overall, we conduct more than 10,000 client-level experiments on 6 Vision-Language FL task settings involving 58 medical image datasets and 4 different VLM architectures of varying sizes to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_1.png} \caption{Distinction of our approach from prior works. (a) illustrates vanilla layer-selection process, which only selects parameter subsets based on the local client data without considering the requirements of the other clients. (b) depicts our approach, $F^3OCUS$, which refines the client-specific layer selection by jointly maximizing overall client-specific importance score and layer selection diversity score across clients.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-008: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have made significant advancements in multi-modal learning, excelling in tasks like Visual Question Answering (VQA) \cite{bordes2024introductionvisionlanguagemodeling,li2021align,kim2021vilt,liu2024improved,li2023blip .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Their effectiveness stems from their extensive parameters often reaching millions or billions, allowing them to learn complex representations of image and text data.
elements.sent-2411-010: Fine-tuning these models with task-specific data is crucial for adapting them to specialized applications.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, gathering diverse training data centrally is challenging, especially in fields like healthcare, where strict privacy regulations prevent data aggregation across different centers.
elements.sent-2411-012: To address the privacy concerns, Federated Learning (FL) \cite{mcmahan2017communication,li2020federated,karimireddy2020scaffold,acar2021federated allows models to be trained directly on local devices, such as in healthcare clinics, without sharing sensitive data.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Yet, fine-tuning large models locally is difficult due to limited computational power and smaller datasets, which hinders VLM adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-014: Balancing privacy with these resource limitations requires innovative solutions like Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) that fine-tunes either selected model parameters or added parameters while keeping the original model fixed \cite{hu2022lora,rebuffi2018efficient,lian2022scaling,ben-zaken-etal-2022-bitfit,frankle2021trainingbatchnormbatchnormexpressive,touvron2022three,lester2021power,VPT_jia,li2021prefix .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Combined with FL, these offer a privacy-preserving and resource-efficient strategy for training large models collaboratively across multiple clients, particularly in computationally constrained settings.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_4.png} \caption{Loss convergence of layer selection methods. The gap between the client-specific NTK and FOCUS demonstrates the importance of our multi-objective meta-heuristic optimization.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-016: Previous research \cite{chen2022fedtune, sun2022exploring,yang2024exploring,yu2023federated,zhuang2023foundation,zhang2024towards has mostly focused on naive combination of centralized finetuning methods with FedAvg \cite{mcmahan2017communication .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, these works are primarily confined to single-modalities, addressing either visual or textual inputs independently.
elements.sent-2411-018: Besides, they do not consider the diverse characteristics and capacities of individual clients and typically assume homogeneous computational resources across all clients which is not applicable in most real-world collaborative settings.
elements.sent-2411-019: Hence, some clients either under-utilize the available resources or are unable to participate due to lack of compute.
elements.sent-2411-020: Our flexible layer selection PEFT-FL framework effectively addresses these issues.
elements.sent-2411-021: The naive combinations of centralized selective PEFT methods \cite{ruckle2020adapterdrop,sung2021training,lee2018snip,lubanagradient,wang2020picking,lee2022surgical and FL only consider local client data and task for selecting parameter subsets without considering other client requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This is especially problematic for FL clients facing challenges like heterogeneous modalities and computes, domain shifts, and statistical heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-023: In such cases, a poorly chosen client-specific configuration can not only slow down the overall convergence (see Fig. 2), but may also perform worse than training each client independently.
elements.sent-2411-024: Achieving the best performance requires a tailored approach that jointly considers client-specific as well as global optimization requirements.
elements.sent-2411-025: To this end, we present a novel framework called $F^3OCUS$ (Federated Finetuning of Foundation Models with Optimal Client-specific Layer Updating Strategy) to improve layer selection by considering both local and global FL characteristics while respecting the client-specific resource constraints.
elements.sent-2411-026: We propose a two-step ``define and refine" procedure at the beginning of every round: (a) client-level strategy, that defines layer importance scores based on the principal eigenvalue of layerwise Neural Tangent Kernel (LNTK) and (b) server-level strategy, that refines client-specific layer selection by maximizing the overall client-specific importance scores while simultaneously minimizing the variance of the histogram of layer selections across clients, thereby promoting a more uniform distribution of layer participation.
elements.sent-2411-028: In order to showcase the effectiveness of $F^3OCUS$, we conduct over 10,000 client-level experiments under 6 Vision-language FL task settings using 4 VLMs and 58 medical image datasets that involve 4 types of heterogeneities based on data, modality, device, and task.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our primary contributions can be summed up as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-030: Dataset contribution: We release Ultra-MedVQA, the largest medical VQA dataset to date, consisting of 707,962 VQA triplets including 9 different modalities and 12 distinct anatomies, covering diverse open-ended and closed questions related to modality, tissue type, image view, anatomy, and disease (see Tab.. 1 for comparison).
elements.sent-2411-031: Technical contributions: We theoretically motivate and propose $F^3OCUS$, a new selective layer fine-tuning strategy.
elements.sent-2411-032: We introduce LNTK-based client-level layer selection and server-level multi-objective meta-heuristic optimization that jointly optimizes client-specific layer importance score and inter-client layer diversity score.
elements.sent-2411-033: We theoretically motivate and analyze the effectiveness of our layer selection strategy and prove its convergence.
elements.sent-2411-034: Empirical contributions: Unlike previous works, we consider more constrained and realistic client settings involving data, modality, task, and device heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-035: We conduct comprehensive evaluations of $F^3OCUS$ with 4 VLMs of varying sizes in diverse FL settings for tuning selective model layers as well as adapters.
elements.sent-2411-036: We empirically show that fine-tuning the last few adapters/layers of VLMs is only as good as tuning randomly selected adapters/layers.
elements.sent-2411-037: Consequently, we present detailed insights into $F^3OCUS$'s performance improvements through: (a) analysis of client and server-based layer rank and importance score computation during training and (b) evaluation of different meta-heuristic optimization algorithms on the server viz., Genetic Algorithm, Artificial Bee Colony, Ant Colony Optimization, Simulated Annealing, and Swarm Particle Optimization.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{\# modalities (M) and VQA triplets in different datasets} 1{ \begin{tabular}{l|cccccc} VQA-RAD SLAKE Path-VQA VQA-Med OmniMedVQA Ours \# M 3 3 2 5 12 9 \# VQA 3515 14028 32799 5500 127995 707962 \end{tabular}} \label{table:comparison}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_2.png} \caption{Visualization of principal eigenvalue magnitudes of LNTK (see $\S 4.1$) for computing layer importance score of LLaVA-1.5-7b} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-038: Federated Learning (FL) FL enables various clients to collaboratively train models in a decentralized manner without sharing local data.
elements.sent-2411-039: The classical FL framework, FedAvg \cite{mcmahan2017communication , offers a practical method for model aggregation.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Several modifications have emerged to address the adverse impact of data heterogeneity in FL \cite{li2020federated,karimireddy2020scaffold,acar2021federated .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Centralized selective fine-tuning: Various methods have been explored for selecting subsets of parameters for fine-tuning foundation models in centralized training.
elements.sent-2411-042: These include optimizing non-structured mask matrices \cite{leemixout,zhang2022fine,xu2021raise,lee2019would,kovaleva2019revealing,zaken2021bitfit,zhang2023crash,shen2021partial , employing layer-wise selection \cite{kovaleva2019revealing,lee2019would,lee2022surgical,kaplun2023less and pruning methods \cite{rachwan2022winning,wang2020picking,lee2018snip,zhao2024pruning,li2020efficient .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Federated selective fine-tuning: Recent research has adapted these selective PEFT methods for FL \cite{nguyen2022begin,chen2022fedtune,hilmkil2021scaling,zhang2022fine .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Specifically, studies by \cite{lee2023layer,dun2022resist explore layer-wise network decomposition to facilitate selective model fine-tuning on client devices.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Partial model personalization algorithms \cite{chen2023efficient,pillutla2022federated aim to train tailored subnetworks on clients to improve local models.}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, these studies do not provide adaptive or dynamic layer selection strategies that consider the diverse characteristics of clients.
elements.sent-2411-047: Unlike prior works, we account for client-specific differences in resources and data distributions while also considering the global optimization requirements to perform selective layer fine-tuning.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_16.png} \caption{Overview of our layer selection strategy, $F^3OCUS$. Each client sends layer importance scores based on the principal eigenvalue of LNTK to the server. The server refines client-specific layer selection by maximizing the cumulative client-specific importance scores while simultaneously minimizing the variance of the histogram of layer selections across clients. It sends the revised layer ranks back.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-048: Consider an FL system with a central server and $N$ clients, represented by $\mathcal{N = \{1, \ldots, N\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Each client has its own private dataset $\mathcal{D _i$, containing $d_i = |\mathcal{D _i|$ data points.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The server contains a pre-trained foundation model parameterized by $\theta \in \mathbb{R ^P$, comprising $L$ layers, indexed by $\mathcal{L = \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The server's objective is to fine-tune this model based on the clients' data $\{\mathcal{D _i\}_{i \in \mathcal{N }$ without directly accessing these datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The learning goal is formalized as: \begin{equation} \min_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^P} F(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i F_i(\theta),\end{equation} where $\alpha_i = \frac{d_i {\sum_{j=1 ^{N d_j}$ denotes relative sample size, and $F_i(\theta) = \frac{1 {d_i \sum_{B_i \in \mathcal{D _i F_i(\theta; B_i)$ represents local training objective for client $i$, with $F_i(\theta; B_i)$ being the (potentially non-convex) loss function of model $\theta$ on data batch $B_i$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The FL training process proceeds over $T$ rounds.
elements.sent-2411-054: In each round $t \in [T]$, the server selects a subset of clients $\mathcal{S _t$ and distributes the updated global model $\theta_t$ to them for training.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Due to resource constraints, instead of the entire model, clients update only a subset of layers during local training.
elements.sent-2411-056: Each client-specific masking vector can be denoted as $m_{i,t \in \{0,1\}^L$, where $m^l_{i,t = 1$ if layer $l$ is selected for training in round $t$, and $m^l_{i,t = 0$ otherwise.}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Thus, the set of selected layers for client $i$ at round $t$ is denoted as $\mathcal{L _{i,t = \{l \in \mathcal{L \mid m^l_{i,t = 1\}$, and the union of all selected layers across clients in round $t$ is $\mathcal{L _t = \bigcup_{i \in \mathcal{S _t \mathcal{L _{i,t}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Clients initialize their local models using global model from the server, $\theta_{i,t}$, and perform $\tau$ steps of local training using mini-batch SGD.
elements.sent-2411-059: For each local step $k \in [\tau]$, client $i$ samples a batch of data $B_{i,t}$ and calculates gradients for the selected layers as: \begin{equation} \sum_{l \in \mathcal{L}^t_i} G_{i,l}(\theta^{k}_{i,t}; B^{k}_{i,t}) = \sum_{l \in \mathcal{L}^t_i} \nabla_l F_i(\theta_{i,t}; B_{i,t}),\end{equation} where $\nabla_l F(\theta)$ denotes the gradient of $F(\theta)$ with respect to the parameters of layer $l$.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\sum_{l \in L^t_i} G_{i,l}(\theta^{k}_{i,t}; B^{k}_{i,t}) = \sum_{l \in L^t_i} \nabla_l F_i(\theta_{i,t}; B_{i,t}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-060: After computing the gradients, the local model is updated with learning rate $\eta$ as: \begin{equation} \theta^{k}_{i,t} = \theta^{k-1}_{i,t} - \eta \sum_{l \in \mathcal{L}^t_i} G_{i}^{l}(\theta^{k-1}_{i,t}; B^{k-1}_{i,t}), \quad \forall k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, \tau\},\end{equation} The accumulated weight update in one local round is: \begin{equation} \delta_{i,t} = \frac{1}{\eta} (\theta_{i,t}^0 - \theta_{i,t}^\tau) = \sum_{k=0}^{\tau-1}\sum_{l \in \mathcal{L}^t_i} G_{i}^{l}(\theta^{k}_{i,t}; B^{k}_{i,t})\end{equation} Accumulated update after federated aggregation on server: \begin{equation} \delta_{t} = \sum_{i \in S_t}\sum_{k=0}^{\tau-1}\sum_{l \in \mathcal{L}^t_i} \alpha_i G_{i}^{l}(\theta^{k}_{i,t}; B^{k}_{i,t})\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: In this section, we first motivate the usage of LNTK towards prioritizing selected layers for client-specific fine-tuning.

elements.sent-2411-063:
The integral NTK of a model \cite{jacot2018neural can be computed as the sum of layerwise NTK (LNTK), where we define LNTK as:  egin{equation} \Theta^l(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}) = 
abla_{	heta^l} F(\mathcal{X}; 	heta^l) 
abla_{	heta^l} F(\mathcal{X}; 	heta^l)^T,\end{equation} where $
abla_{	heta^l F(\mathcal{X ; 	heta^l)$ denotes the Jacobian of $F$ at input points $\mathcal{X}$ with respect to the $l$-th layer parameters $	heta^l$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Since $\Theta^l(\mathcal{X , \mathcal{X )$ is a positive semi-definite real symmetric matrix, we perform an eigen-decomposition of LNTK as: \begin{equation} \Theta^l(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{X}) = U^l \Lambda^l (U^l)^T = \sum_{j=1}^{nk} \lambda^l_j u^l_j (u^l_j)^T\end{equation} where $\Lambda^l = \operatorname{diag (\lambda^l_1, \lambda^l_2, \dots, \lambda^l_{nk )$ contains eigenvalues $\lambda^l_j$ of $\Theta^l(\mathcal{X , \mathcal{X )$, and each $\lambda^l_j \geq 0$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The mean output of the $l$-th layer in the eigenbasis of the LNTK can be described by: \begin{equation} (U^lE[\mathbf{f}^l(\mathcal{X})])_j = \left(I - e^{-\eta \lambda_j^l t}\right)(U^ly^l)_j\end{equation} where $\mathbf{f ^l(\mathcal{X )$ and $y^l$ are actual and target $l$-th layer outputs.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: This formulation demonstrates that the convergence behavior of a layer is largely influenced by the eigenvalues $\lambda_j^l$ of LNTK.
elements.sent-2411-068: Let $\lambda^l_{1 \geq \lambda^l_{2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda^l_{n}$ denote the eigenvalues of $\Theta^l(\mathcal{X , \mathcal{X )$, where $\lambda^l_{1}$ is the principal eigenvalue.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: In particular, $\lambda_1^l$ plays a dominant role in the convergence dynamics \cite{bowman2023brief .}
elements.sent-2411-070: When using the largest possible layer-specific learning rate, $\eta^l \sim \frac{2 {\lambda_1^l}$, the training process aligns most strongly with the direction associated with the principal eigenvalue as the NTK eigenvectors corresponding to the principal eigenvalue are learned quicker due to spectral bias \cite{rahaman2019spectral,bowman2023brief,cao2019towards,shi2024train .}}
elements.sent-2411-071: This motivates the use of the principal eigenvalue $\lambda^l_{1}$ in our client-specific layer importance score, as it represents the maximum alignment of each layer's parameter space with the client's data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-072: This provides a principled basis for prioritizing these layers.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_3.png} \caption{Visualization of layer ranks of LLaVA-1.5 across rounds in different clients based on LNTK. Darker color implies higher rank.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-074: Assuming that the true labels align well with the top eigenvectors as discussed earlier, i.e., $\left( (u^l_j)^T Y \right)^2$ is large for large $\lambda^l_j$, directional derivative of the loss function can be regarded as closely related to the eigenspectrum of the layerwise NTKs.
elements.sent-2411-075: Specifically, (vi) suggests that layers with higher principal eigenvalues contribute more significantly to loss reduction during training.
elements.sent-2411-076: Overall, Eq. 12 suggests that the loss decreases more rapidly along the eigenspaces corresponding to larger LNTK eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-077: Given that the principal eigenvalue $\lambda^l_1$ vary across layers as seen in Fig. 3, we propose to selectively fine-tune layers with larger $\lambda^l_1$ to achieve efficient learning with limited computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-078: Therefore, we define the client-specific layer importance score $S^l_i = \frac{\lambda^l_{i, 1 {\sum_{k=1 ^L \lambda^k_{i, 1 }$ where the sum in the denominator normalizes the principal eigenvalue across all layers, ensuring that $S^l_i$ captures the relative importance of each layer for client $i$ in terms of its contribution to the model’s predictive capacity for that client's data distribution.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: This formulation prioritizes layers whose NTK principal eigenvalues dominate, indicating strong client-specific parameter alignment (see Figs.. 3 \& 5).
elements.sent-2411-081: We begin with some necessary assumptions following previous works \cite{wang2020tackling,karimireddy2020scaffold and then introduce a set of assumptions to analyze the impact of the layers selected using LNTK: Assumption 1: ($\gamma$-Smoothness) There exists a constant $\gamma > 0$ such that for any $\theta, \phi \in \mathbb{R ^p$: \begin{equation} \|\nabla F_i(\theta) - \nabla F_i(\phi)\|_2 \leq \gamma \|\theta - \phi\|_2, \quad \forall i \in \mathcal{N}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Assumption 2: (Unbiased and variance-bounded stochastic gradient) The layerwise stochastic gradient \( G_{i,l (\theta_t; B_{i,t ) \) computed on a randomly sampled data batch \( B_{i,t \) serves as an unbiased estimate of the layerwise full-batch gradient: \begin{equation} \mathbb{E}_{B_{i,t}}[G_{i,l}(\theta_t; B_{i,t})] = \nabla F_{i,l}(\theta_t).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Besides, there exist constants $\sigma_l > 0, \forall l \in \mathcal{L}$ such that $\mathbb{E _{B_{i,t \|G_{i ^{l (\theta_t; B_{i,t ) - \nabla F_{i ^{l (\theta_t)\|^2 \leq \sigma_l^2, \forall i \in \mathcal{N}$ and $\sum_{l \in \mathcal{L \sigma_l^2 \leq \sigma^2$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Assumption 3: (Gradient Diversity) The non-IID client data distribution causes diverse gradients.
elements.sent-2411-085: There exist constants $\kappa_l > 0, \forall l \in \mathcal{L}$ such that: \begin{equation} \mathbb{E}_{B_{i,t}} [\|\nabla F(\theta_t) - \nabla F_{i}^{l}(\theta_t; B_{i,t})\|^2] \leq \kappa_l^2, \forall i \in \mathcal{N}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-086: In contrast to the theoretical analysis for standard FL settings, our LNTK-based fine-tuning introduces three additional challenges: (i) Each client only updates a subset of layers.

abla F_{i ^{l (	heta_t) 
eq 
abla F_{i (	heta_t)$ where equality holds only if all layers are selected.}}}}}}

The aggregated gradient of the selected layers is not equal to the gradient computed based on the global loss function i.e., $\sum_{i \in \mathcal{S ^t \sum_{l \in \mathcal{L _t \alpha_{i,t 
abla_l F_i(	heta_t) 
eq \sum_{l \in \mathcal{L _t 
abla_l F(	heta_t),$ where equality holds only if all clients select the same layers.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: So, LNTK-based PEFT-FL converges to a small neighbourhood of a stationary point of standard FL, maintaining a non-zero error floor.. See Suppl.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=2\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_7.png} \caption{Visualization of refined layer ranks of LLaVA-1.5 based on $F^3OCUS$. Comparing it with Fig. 5 shows that the server-level meta-heuristic optimization refines the client-level layer ranks by increasing inter-client layer diversity at every round.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-091: To explicitly examine the impact of potential noise introduced by LNTK-based layer selection as well the variation of selection count across clients, we reformulate the convergence in Theorem 2 leveraging two additional assumptions: Assumption 4: (Bounded stochastic gradient) The expected squared norm of stochastic gradients is bounded uniformly, i.e., for constant $\sigma_g > 0$ and any $i,t$: \begin{equation} \mathbb{E}_{B_{i,t}} [\| G_i(\theta_t;B_{i,t}) \|^2 ] \leq \sigma_g^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-092: Assumption 5: (Normalized Layer Selection Noise Bound) There exist some $\xi^2 \in [0, 1)$ and any $t, i$, the normalized layer selection noise due to LNTK is bounded by: \begin{equation} \frac{\| \theta_t - \hat{\theta}_{t,i}\|^2}{\| \theta_t \|^2} \leq \xi^2\end{equation} where $\hat{\theta _{t,i}$ denotes LNTK-based client-specific layers.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Theorem 2 (Impact of layer selection-based noise $\xi$ and variance of selection count $s_d^2$ on LNTK convergence): \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \min_{t \in [T]} \mathbb{E} \left[ \left\| \nabla F(\theta_t) \right\|_2^2 \right] \leq \frac{2}{(\eta - 3\gamma \eta^2) T} \Bigg[ F(\theta^0) - F(\theta^*) + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\eta \xi^2 \gamma^2 N s_d}{2} \left( 1 + 3 \eta \gamma \right) \mathbb{E} \left[ \left\| \sum_{t=1}^T \nabla_\ell F(\theta_t) \right\|^2 \right] \quad + \frac{\gamma \eta^2 N T s_d}{2} \left(\eta \gamma \sigma_g^2 (1 + 3 \gamma) + 3 \sigma^2 s_d\right) \Bigg] \text{s.t.} \quad \theta^* = \arg \min_{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p} F(\theta) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Remark 2: With $\eta \leq \text{min}\{\frac{1 {\sqrt{T ,\frac{1 {6\gamma}\}$, model converges to a neighborhood of a stationary point of FL with a small gap due to layer selection-based noise $\xi$ and variance of selection count $s_d$ over clients.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation} \min_{t \in [T]} E \left[ \left\| \nabla F(\theta_t) \right\|_2^2 \right] \leq \frac{2}{(\eta - 3\gamma \eta^2) T} \Bigg[ F(\theta^0) - F(\theta^*) + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \frac{\eta \xi^2 \gamma^2 N s_d}{2} \left( 1 + 3 \eta \gamma \right) E \left[ \left\| \sum_{t=1}^T \nabla_\ell F(\theta_t) \right\|^2 \right] \quad + \frac{\gamma \eta^2 N T s_d}{2} \left(\eta \gamma \sigma_g^2 (1 + 3 \gamma) + 3 \sigma^2 s_d\right) \Bigg] s.t. \quad \theta^* = \arg \min_{\theta \in R^p} F(\theta) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-094: This motivates us to jointly minimize the influence of $\xi$ and $s_d$ for better convergence.
elements.sent-2411-095: Motivated by this, we refine the selected layers on server (see Fig. 6) to achieve two primary objectives (see Eq. 21): maximizing the cumulative client-specific importance scores based on LNTK and minimizing the variance of layer selection histogram (see Fig. 7) to encourage more balanced usage of each layer over clients.
elements.sent-2411-096: Let \( n_l \) represent the number of clients that select layer \( l \), with \( \bar{n =\frac{1 {L \sum_{l=1 ^{L n_l\) as the mean count of layer usage.. (See Suppl.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The constraint incorporates client-specific computational budget $L_{\text{i,max }$.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=1\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_10.png} \caption{Layer selection histogram shows the impact of server-level optimization. It encourages more layers to participate (see blue-marked areas) by maximizing layer-selection diversity i.e., reducing the variance of the selection count (vertical axis).} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-098: Traditional optimization methods struggle to optimize these two conflicting objectives.
elements.sent-2411-099: Neural networks cannot be employed to optimize due to absence of data on server.
elements.sent-2411-100: Besides, the number of possible configurations is particularly high due to the large number of layers in foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since this problem involves multiple objectives, there is no "best" solution but rather a set of optimal trade-offs (the Pareto front).
elements.sent-2411-102: Meta-heuristic algorithms are well-suited to explore such complex, high-dimensional solution spaces in absence of training data by balancing exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-103: To this end, we carefully select and investigate 5 meta-heuristic algorithms spanning all 3 algorithm categories: evolutionary, physics-based, and swarm-based.. See Suppl.
elements.sent-2411-104: Each of the following algorithms aim to maximize client-specific importance while ensuring balanced layer utilization across clients based on their underlying principles: (1) Non-Dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm (NSGA): We use NSGA \cite{deb2002fast to iteratively evolve a population of layer selections.}
elements.sent-2411-105: We initialize population based on client-specific importance scores while incorporating probability-based sampling for broader search space coverage.
elements.sent-2411-106: This guided randomness helps balance exploration (diversifying choices) and exploitation (prioritizing high-importance layers).
elements.sent-2411-107: Genetic operations like crossover and mutation generate new solutions, while non-dominated sorting and crowding distance ensure diversity on the Pareto Front.
elements.sent-2411-108: The optimization proceeds in three phases: Employed bees exploit local solutions, adjusting layer assignments based on importance scores and diversity, occasionally accepting worse solutions to escape local optima.
elements.sent-2411-109: Onlooker bees then select solutions based on a probability weighted by importance and diversity, while Scout bees abandon unproductive solutions, replacing them with randomly generated ones to promote exploration.
elements.sent-2411-110: Non-dominated solutions are stored in a Pareto archive, which guides refinement over iterations.
elements.sent-2411-111: The Pareto archive preserves non-dominated solutions.
elements.sent-2411-112: Pheromone trails are then updated, with evaporation to prevent stale paths and additional pheromone deposits on layers selected, thereby encouraging their selection in subsequent iterations.
elements.sent-2411-113: It accepts new configurations if it offers higher importance or lower variance based on Eq. 21.
elements.sent-2411-114: If the new configuration is less optimal, it may still be accepted based on a probability that decreases with the temperature allowing SA to avoid being trapped in local optima.
elements.sent-2411-115: As the temperature cools, SA gradually refines the search, focusing on fine-tuning layer assignments that respect both client-specific importance and a balanced distribution of layer usage.
elements.sent-2411-116: Randomized probability-based sampling is used to ensure diverse initial positions, promoting exploration of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-117: Each particle updates its velocity and position by balancing three influences: inertia (maintaining its current layer selection), a cognitive component (best individual solution), and a social component (globally optimal solution).

elements.sent-2411-130: We plot the loss curves of LNTK and $F^3OCUS$ and compare them with several pruning methods in Fig. 2, which demonstrates the impact of our server-level optimization on faster convergence.
elements.sent-2411-131: We also visualize the principal eigenvalue magnitudes of all 32 layers of LLaVA across different clients at the beginning of a round for Task 1 in Fig. 3 and show the layer ranks over all rounds in Fig. 5.
elements.sent-2411-132: Consequently, the effect of server-level optimization on overall layer selections is shown in Fig. 6 using relative layer ranks and in Fig. 7 using the layer selection histogram.
elements.sent-2411-133: In Fig. 9, we motivate the current work by revealing that, contrary to common belief, fine-tuning the last 'K' layers is only as effective as fine-tuning random 'K' layers.
elements.sent-2411-134: Table 4 shows the comparable performance of different meta-heuristic algorithms for all clients in Task 2.
elements.sent-2411-135: For the 'microscopy' client in the same task, we present t-SNE feature visualizations in Fig. 10 for (a) Federated Dropout (labeled random), (b) LNTK, and (c) $F^3OCUS$, highlighting the striking improvement in generating distinctive feature representations.. See Suppl.
elements.sent-2411-136: We presented $F^3OCUS$, a novel and theoretically grounded approach for federated fine-tuning of Vision-Language foundation models in client-specific resource-constrained settings.
elements.sent-2411-137: Our server-level multi-objective meta-heuristic optimization scheme does not require any data on server and can be easily combined with any existing layer selection or pruning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-138: Additionally, we released Ultra-MedVQA, the largest medical VQA dataset, covering 12 anatomies, for supporting further VLM research.
elements.sent-2411-139: Experimental results demonstrate that $F^3OCUS$ achieves superior performance across multiple vision-language tasks and models, providing a practical solution for fine-tuning large foundation models in decentralized environments.Our code and dataset will be made available upon acceptance.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-140: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-142: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-143: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-144: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-145: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-146: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-147: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-148: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-149: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-150: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-151: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-152: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-153: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-154: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-155: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-156: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-157: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-158: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-161: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-162: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-163: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-164: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-165: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-166: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-167: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-017: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-169: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-172: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-173: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-174: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-175: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-176: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_1.png} \caption{Distinction of our approach from prior works. (a) illustrates vanilla layer-selection process, which only selects parameter subsets based on the local client data without considering the requirements of the other clients. (b) depicts our approach, \textbf{F\textsuperscript{3}OCUS}, which refines the client-specific layer selection by jointly maximizing overall client-specific importance score and layer selection diversity score across clients.} \label{fig:11}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_4.png} \caption{Loss convergence of layer selection methods. The gap between the client-specific NTK and FOCUS demonstrates the importance of our multi-objective meta-heuristic optimization.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-177: Besides, they do not consider the diverse characteristics and capacities of individual clients and typically assume homogeneous computational resources across all clients \cite{saha2024fedpia,chen2024feddat which is not applicable in most real-world collaborative settings.}
elements.sent-2411-178: To this end, we present a novel framework called F\textsuperscript{3OCUS (Federated Finetuning of Foundation Models with Optimal Client-specific Layer Updating Strategy) to improve layer selection by considering both local and global FL characteristics while respecting the client-specific resource constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-179: In order to showcase the effectiveness of F\textsuperscript{3OCUS , we conduct over 10,000 client-level experiments under 6 Vision-language FL task settings using 4 VLMs and 58 medical image datasets that involve 4 types of heterogeneities based on data, modality, device, and task.}
elements.sent-2411-180: Technical contributions: We theoretically motivate and propose F\textsuperscript{3OCUS , a new selective layer fine-tuning strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-181: We conduct comprehensive evaluations of F\textsuperscript{3OCUS with 4 VLMs of varying sizes in diverse FL settings for tuning selective layers.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Consequently, we present detailed insights into F\textsuperscript{3OCUS 's performance improvements through: (a) analysis of client and server-based layer rank and importance score computation during training and (b) evaluation of different meta-heuristic optimization algorithms on the server viz., Genetic Algorithm, Artificial Bee Colony, Ant Colony Optimization, Simulated Annealing, and Swarm Particle Optimization.}
elements.figure-2411-020: \caption{\# modalities (M) and VQA triplets in different datasets} .6{ \begin{tabular}{l|cccccc} \textbf{VQA-RAD \cite{lau2018dataset}} \textbf{SLAKE \cite{liu2021slake}} \textbf{Path-VQA \cite{he2020pathvqa}} \textbf{VQA-Med \cite{BenAbacha2020VQAMed}} \textbf{OmniMedVQA \cite{Hu_2024_CVPR}} Ours \# M 3 3 2 5 12 9 \# VQA 3515 14028 32799 5500 127995 707962 \end{tabular}} \label{table:comparison}
elements.sent-2411-183: Several modifications have emerged to address the adverse impact of data heterogeneity in FL \cite{li2020federated,karimireddy2020scaffold,acar2021federated,saha2023rethinking,saha2024examiningmodalityincongruitymultimodal,wagner2023post,wagner2024feasibility,hernandez2024review .}
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=2.0\columnwidth]{sec/CVPR_2025_16.png} \caption{Overview of our layer selection strategy, \textbf{F\textsuperscript{3}OCUS}. Each client sends layer importance scores based on the principal eigenvalue of LNTK to the server. The server refines client-specific layer selection by maximizing the cumulative client-specific importance scores while simultaneously minimizing the variance of the histogram of layer selections across clients. It sends the revised layer ranks back.} \label{fig:11}
elements.sent-2411-184: We first motivate the usage of LNTK towards prioritizing selected layers for client-specific fine-tuning.

elements.sent-2411-197: We presented F\textsuperscript{3OCUS , a novel and theoretically grounded approach for federated fine-tuning of Vision-Language foundation models in client-specific resource-constrained settings.}
elements.sent-2411-198: F\textsuperscript{3OCUS effectively balances individual client requirements with collective layer diversity thereby improving model convergence and performance accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Experimental results demonstrate that F\textsuperscript{3OCUS achieves superior performance across multiple vision-language tasks and models, providing a practical solution for fine-tuning large foundation models in decentralized environments.}

elements.sent-2411-200: This work was supported in part by the UK EPSRC (Engineering and Physical Research Council) Programme Grant EP/T028572/1 (VisualAI), a UK EPSRC Doctoral Training Partnership award, the UKRI grant EP/X040186/1 (Turing AI Fellowship), and the InnoHK-funded Hong Kong Centre for Cerebro-cardiovascular Health Engineering (COCHE) Project 2.1 (Cardiovascular risks in early life and fetal echocardiography).
elements.sent-2411-201: FW is supported by the EPSRC Centre for Doctoral Training in Health Data Science (EP/S02428X/1), by the Anglo-Austrian Society, and by an Oxford-Reuben scholarship.. D.A.C.
